module.title: Module 4: Locating Sequential Conveyances
module.description: How surveyors and courts locate boundaries created one conveyance after another over time, applying senior/junior rights, the dignity (priority) of conflicting calls, and the rule that the original surveyor's footsteps and the parties' intent control.
learning_objectives: Explain the basic elements courts have developed to resolve conflicts in deeds.
Differentiate between sequential and simultaneous conveyances and the discrepancy-resolution method each requires.
Apply the order of importance (dignity) for conflicting boundary elements in different scenarios.
Compute corrected positions, remainders, and gap/overlap widths arising from sequential conveyances using the controlling call.
module.textbook_chapters: Brown ch. 9 (Locating Sequential Conveyances); see also ch. 7

lessons.title: Resolving Conflicts in Deeds: The Three Basic Elements
learning_objectives: State the three elements courts use to resolve deed conflicts and rank them.
Explain which of those elements a surveyor may and may not apply.
Describe the surveyor's duty to follow the footsteps of the original surveyor.

content.html:
Why deeds conflict

A deed is a written attempt to describe a unique piece of ground using words, numbers, monuments, and references. Because the land is conveyed one parcel at a time over years and decades, by different scriveners using different instruments and different surveys, the calls in adjoining deeds frequently overlap, leave gaps, or contradict each other internally. The job of the retracing surveyor is not to redesign the boundary but to discover where it was originally created and to honor the rights that attached when each parcel was severed. To do that, courts and surveyors apply a settled set of priorities.

The three elements courts use

Courts have developed three basic elements for resolving conflicts in deeds, applied in this order of authority:

  • 1. Unwritten / possession rights. An occupancy right that has ripened into a legal right (adverse possession, acquiescence, estoppel, agreement, acts of nature) can alter or even overcome a written conveyance.
  • 2. Senior rights. As between private parties, a senior right is superior to a junior right. The first parcel out of a common tract gets the full measure of its description; the later parcel takes the remainder.
  • 3. Written intentions of the parties. The expressed intent of the parties, found within the four corners of the instrument, is paramount in construing what the writing means.

content.text: The surveyor's lane: A surveyor does NOT determine the validity of occupancy or property rights, so a surveyor cannot apply element 1 (unwritten rights). A surveyor can and must apply elements 2 (senior rights) and 3 (written intent). When unwritten rights appear to exist, the surveyor discloses the facts and advises the client to seek legal advice — only a court can reform the boundary.

content.html:
Following the footsteps of the original surveyor

The cardinal rule of retracement is that the surveyor must follow in the footsteps of the original surveyor — locate the lines, corners, and monuments where they were actually run and set when the parcel was created. The retracing surveyor's duty is to locate the boundaries described in the operative muniments of title, not to impose a mathematically perfect modern survey. As Hermansen puts it, the original surveyor's marks and monuments are not by themselves determinative unless they are given recognition in the operative description. A more accurate modern measurement does not relocate an original corner that can still be identified on the ground.

sections.title: Case: Stafford v. King, 30 Tex. 257 (1867)
sections.content: The foundational Texas authority on both the dignity of calls and the footsteps doctrine. The Texas Supreme Court held: "The actual identification of the survey, the footsteps of the surveyor upon the ground, should always be followed, by whatever rule they may be traced." The court also stated the reliability order as natural objects, then artificial objects, then course and distance, while cautioning that the construction adopted must be the one most consistent with the intention apparent on the face of the patent.

content.text: Texas note: Because every Texas title traces to an original GLO survey/patent (Republic- or State-era), and the law required a survey before a patent could issue, Texas is an especially strong "footsteps" jurisdiction. Retracing the original Texas surveyor's actual work is the dominant methodology, with dignity-of-calls and proportioning used as fallbacks.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Courts resolve deed conflicts with three elements, in order: unwritten/possession rights, senior rights, then written intent.
A surveyor may apply senior rights and written intent but may NOT decide unwritten/possession rights — that is for a court.
The footsteps of the original surveyor, where identifiable, control over later 'more accurate' measurements.
Stafford v. King (1867) is the seminal Texas case for both the footsteps rule and the dignity of calls.

flashcards.definition: The principle that a retracing surveyor must locate boundaries where the original surveyor actually ran the lines and set the corners; identifiable original monuments control over later measurements.
flashcards.hint_1: "Follow in the footsteps of the original surveyor" — Stafford v. King.

flashcards.definition: As between private parties, the earlier-created (senior) conveyance is superior to a later (junior) one; the senior takes the full measure of its description and the junior takes the remainder.

A document emanating from a grantor or authority that had both the title and the power to fix a boundary, and therefore operates to create an authoritative boundary (as opposed to a merely recorded but powerless document).

flashcards.term: Three elements for resolving deed conflicts
flashcards.definition: (1) Unwritten/possession rights that have ripened into legal title, (2) senior rights, (3) written intentions of the parties — applied in that order of authority.

images_needed.caption: A retracing surveyor finding an original iron pipe offset from a modern 'perfect' computed position, with an arrow showing the boundary held at the found monument (footsteps control).

lessons.title: Sequential vs. Simultaneous Conveyances
learning_objectives: Define sequential and simultaneous conveyances and give an example of each.
Explain who gets overlaps and gaps under each scheme.
Apply senior rights to sequential conveyances and apportionment to simultaneous conveyances.

content.html:
Two ways parcels come into being

The method used to resolve a discrepancy depends entirely on how the parcels were created. There are two paradigms:

  • Sequential conveyances come into being because of a time lapse between conveyance instruments. A landowner carves off one tract, then later carves off another from the remaining land. These involve senior and junior rights.
  • Simultaneous conveyances are several parcels created at the same instant from a common scheme — classically, the lots of a recorded subdivision plat. All lots have equal standing (no senior rights among them).

rows: One after another, with a time lapse
No lot is senior to another
Junior absorbs deficiency; gap belongs to seller (may be the State)
Junior absorbs excess (senior made whole first)

content.html:
Overlaps and gaps in sequential conveyances

In a sequential scheme the rule is mechanical and not prorated: the senior is made whole first, and the junior takes whatever is left. Therefore an overlap (two deeds describing the same ground) belongs to the senior parcel. A gap or hiatus (ground described by neither deed) belongs to the original grantor/seller — and where the grantor was the sovereign, the gap may belong to the State. Excess or deficiency between controlling monuments is dumped entirely on the junior parcel; it is never split between senior and junior.

content.text: Be careful: 'simultaneous' lots can still hide a senior right. If an original tract that was senior to others later becomes a subdivision, the senior right from the original tract survives. A title search back to the original formation of the conveyance is necessary for a correct solution.

content.html:
Apportionment in simultaneous conveyances

Because no subdivision lot is senior, any excess or shortage found between controlling, undisputed monuments is distributed among the interior lots in proportion to their record lengths. No single lot absorbs the whole error. This is the PLSS/BLM idea of single proportionate measurement on a line, and double proportionate measurement for a corner controlled in two directions. Apportionment is a last resort: found monuments and original corners control first, and an isolable gross blunder is placed where it belongs rather than smeared across all lots.

content.text: Texas differs (PLSS): Single/double proportionate measurement are federal-PLSS/BLM constructs. Texas retained its own public lands and has no township-range grid, so Texas leans on original-survey footsteps and uses proportioning sparingly. There is no controlling Texas Supreme Court opinion adopting 'single/double proportionate measurement' by name; in Texas it is established surveying practice introduced through expert testimony, not a marquee case.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Sequential = parcels carved out over time → senior/junior rights govern; junior absorbs excess/deficiency.
Simultaneous = subdivision lots created at one instant → no seniority; excess/deficiency apportioned proportionately.
Overlaps go to the senior parcel; gaps go to the seller (possibly the State).
A title search to the original conveyance is required because a 'simultaneous' lot can carry a hidden senior right.
In Texas, proportioning is a genuine fallback behind original-survey footsteps.

flashcards.definition: Parcels created one after another with a time lapse between instruments; resolved by senior/junior rights, with the junior absorbing any excess or deficiency.
Several parcels created at the same instant from a common scheme (e.g., a recorded subdivision plat); all lots have equal standing and excess/deficiency is apportioned.
A strip of ground described by neither of two adjoining sequential deeds; it belongs to the original grantor/seller, which may be the State.
Ground described by two conflicting deeds. In a sequential scheme the overlap belongs to the senior parcel.
Restoring a lost corner on a single line between two found monuments so that the corrected interval bears the same ratio to the corrected whole as the record interval bears to the record whole.
Restoring a lost corner controlled in two directions from four surrounding found monuments, proportioning independently in each direction.

images_needed.caption: Plan view of two sequential deeds sharing an overlap band, with the overlap shaded and labeled 'belongs to senior'; a separate gap band labeled 'belongs to seller / State'.
A platted block of equal lots between two found block corners, showing record total vs. measured total and the surplus distributed proportionally to each lot.

lessons.title: Senior and Junior Rights in Practice
learning_objectives: Explain the principle underlying senior rights and how a junior deed is cut back.
Evaluate when a call for an adjoiner reliably indicates seniority.
Describe how ambiguities in state patents are construed.

content.html:
The senior-rights principle

Where two parties are given title to the same parcel and possession is not a consideration, the party with senior rights has the right of possession under common law; the junior deed gets what is left over. The rationale is simple: once a grantor has sold land to the senior grantee, the grantor no longer owns it and cannot resell the same rights to a junior grantee. Seniority does not expand a grant beyond its own description — it only resolves overlaps and gaps in the senior's favor.

Calls for an adjoiner and seniority

An abutter (adjoiner) call — a call to the line of an adjoining tract — is normally considered senior, because the adjoining tract usually existed first. But a call for an adjoiner is not always a correct criterion for determining seniority. The adjoiner may itself be junior, or the call may be informational. Only a title search back to the original formation of the conveyance reliably establishes which parcel is senior.

content.text: Worked logic: Grantor O owns a 400-ft frontage. In 2010 O deeds the west 250 ft to A (senior). In 2015 O deeds 'the remainder, being 150 ft, more or less' to B (junior). A resurvey finds the true frontage is only 380 ft. A still gets the full 250 ft (senior). B is cut back to 380 - 250 = 130 ft. The 20-ft deficiency falls entirely on the junior, B.

content.html:
Ambiguities in state patents

A surprising reversal applies to grants from the State. Ambiguities in patents issued by a state are resolved in favor of the state, because patents are treated as quitclaim-type deeds that give no warranty. This is the opposite of the usual private-transaction rule, under which an ambiguous grant is construed against the grantor and in favor of the grantee.

content.text: Texas overlay: TBPELS rules (22 Tex. Admin. Code Ch. 138) affirmatively require Texas surveyors to honor junior/senior rights and the record chain during retracement. GLO patents create the senior title against which later subdivisions are junior. Confirm the exact section (≈ Subch. E) before citing it in writing.

content.html:
Reservations and the grantor

The general construction rule favors the grantee, but a reservation in a grant, and every grant by a public officer or body to a private party, is interpreted in favor of the grantor. Certain and definite statements prevail; erroneous or ambiguous terms may be rejected — but such rejections should be kept as few as possible so that the instrument is given effect.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: A grantor cannot resell rights already conveyed; the junior deed takes only the remainder.
Excess or deficiency falls entirely on the junior parcel, not prorated with the senior.
Abutter calls are normally senior but are not a conclusive test — search title to the original conveyance.
Ambiguities in state patents are resolved for the State (patents are quitclaim, no warranty) — the reverse of private deeds.
A reservation, and any public grant to a private party, is construed in favor of the grantor.

flashcards.definition: The junior grantee receives only what the grantor still owned after the senior conveyance — 'what is left over' — absorbing any overlap loss or deficiency.
A call in a deed to the boundary of an adjoining tract; normally treated as senior, but not a conclusive indicator of seniority without a title search.
Ambiguities in a state-issued patent are resolved in favor of the State, because patents are quitclaim deeds giving no warranty — opposite of the private-deed rule.
In private transactions an ambiguous grant is read in favor of the grantee; but a reservation, and any public grant to a private party, is read in favor of the grantor.

images_needed.caption: Frontage strip showing senior parcel A holding its full record width and junior parcel B reduced to the remainder, with the deficiency annotated on B.

lessons.title: The Dignity (Priority) of Conflicting Calls
learning_objectives: List the order of importance for conflicting boundary elements.
Distinguish natural monuments, artificial monuments, and adjoiner calls.
Explain how intent and the footsteps doctrine can override the mechanical hierarchy.

content.html:
Why an order of importance exists

When the calls inside one description conflict, the law ranks them by their relative reliability — their "dignity." The logic is the least-likely-mistake principle: adopt the description element that is least likely to be affected by mistakes. A river is hard to misplace; a recorded distance is easy to mistype. The hierarchy is therefore a set of rebuttable presumptions of evidence, not iron rules.

rows: Possession, agreement, prescription; senior rights in sequential conveyances (can overcome the writing)
Rivers, creeks, lakes, mountains, rocks, marked trees of natural origin
Set iron pipes, concrete monuments, stakes, fence corners, centerlines of roads, rock piles
An already-established senior survey line called for and identified
Acreage — weakest; 'of no description' by itself
Computed/interpolated; inferior to the elements they were computed from

content.text: Brown / lecture ordering: 1) Rights of possession & unwritten transfer; 2) Senior rights (overlaps); 3) Written intentions — call for survey, natural monuments, then artificial monuments; 4) Distance or direction; 5) Area; 6) Coordinates and calculated/interpolated evidence. Note that the 'adjoiner' tier is a modern Texas-treatise refinement inserted between artificial monuments and course.

content.html:
Natural vs. artificial monuments

Per Stafford v. King: "Natural objects are mountains, lakes, rivers, creeks, rocks, and the like. Artificial objects are marked lines, trees, stakes, etc." Natural monuments outrank artificial ones because they are larger, more permanent, and less easily moved or fabricated. For any monument to be controlling it must be called for, identifiable, and undisturbed. A monument set after the deed was written (an uncalled-for monument) does not control the boundary, though it may be evidence of unwritten rights.

Intent overrides the hierarchy

The hierarchy always yields to the intent of the parties and to the footsteps of the original surveyor. A call that would defeat the parties' intentions is rejected regardless of its comparative dignity. A call to a monument, though normally controlling, may be rejected where all other terms in the deed show the monument call was inserted in error. Each term must be read in the light of all the other terms. Stafford's own framing is conditional: adopt the construction "most consistent with the intention apparent upon the face of the patent."

content.text: Texas differs / emphasizes: Texas fully adopts the natural → artificial → adjoiner → course → distance → quantity → coordinates ordering, but Stafford v. King imposes two caveats that come first: (1) the intent of the parties is paramount, and (2) the footsteps of the original surveyor, where ascertainable on the ground, override even the field-note calls. The location of a survey line is ultimately a question of fact for the factfinder.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Order of dignity: senior/unwritten rights > natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoiners > course > distance > area > coordinates.
The hierarchy reflects the least-likely-mistake principle and is rebuttable, not absolute.
A monument must be called for, identifiable, and undisturbed to control.
Intent and the original surveyor's footsteps override the mechanical ranking — a call defeating intent is rejected regardless of its dignity.
Area/quantity and coordinates are the weakest elements.

flashcards.definition: The ranked reliability of conflicting deed elements: natural monuments > artificial monuments > adjoiner calls > course > distance > area > coordinates, all subordinate to intent and footsteps.
A naturally occurring boundary object such as a river, creek, lake, mountain, or rock; highest dignity among physical calls.
A human-made boundary marker such as an iron pipe, concrete monument, stake, fence corner, or road centerline; ranks below natural monuments.
When deed elements conflict, adopt the element least likely to be affected by mistakes; the rationale behind the dignity hierarchy.
A monument set after the deed was written; it does not control the boundary but may evidence possible unwritten rights.

A controlling call prevails in conflict; an informative call merely helps locate the controlling element (e.g., 'North 200.00 ft to a 2-inch iron pipe' — the pipe controls, the bearing/distance inform).

images_needed.caption: A vertical ladder/pyramid diagram of the dignity of calls from senior rights at the top down to coordinates at the bottom, with examples beside each rung.
A line 'North 200.00 ft to a 2-inch iron pipe' where the found pipe is at 203.4 ft; arrow shows the corner held at the pipe (monument controls over distance).

lessons.title: Reading the Words: Rules of Construction, Parol Evidence, and Errors
learning_objectives: Apply the principal rules of construction and maxims of jurisprudence to a deed.
State when parol evidence may and may not be used.
Locate and place an isolable error within a description.

content.html:
Construe the whole instrument

When a conveyance is reduced to writing it is assumed to contain all the terms intended and is construed, if possible, to give effect to all the terms. The intentions of the parties as expressed by the writing are paramount. Several controlling maxims of jurisprudence recur in land disputes:

  • Between rights otherwise equal, the earliest is preferred (the seniority principle).
  • Particular expressions qualify general ones — a specific metes-and-bounds description controls over a vague general one.
  • An interpretation that gives effect is preferred to one that makes the deed void.
  • One who grants a thing is presumed to grant whatever is essential to its use.

Controlling vs. informative terms

Within a single call, some words control and others merely inform. In the call "North 200.00' to a 2" iron pipe", the iron pipe controls (a found, called-for, undisturbed monument); the bearing and distance inform where to look for it. But if the pipe is lost, the bearing and distance become controlling. The word "to" is a word of exclusion: the call preceding "to" is informative and the call after "to" is controlling. General terms such as "near" are informative and yield to the specific call, while unrestricted general terms like "northerly" or "about 20 chains" become exact if nothing restricts them.

Parol evidence: a narrow door

Boundaries must be established in accordance with the written terms of the deed. Parol (oral) evidence may NOT be used to add, subtract, or vary the terms of a deed. It may be used only to:

  • establish the location of an ambiguous Point of Beginning;
  • show where the line was actually run and marked; and
  • show fraud.

In short, parol evidence explains an ambiguity in the writing; it does not rewrite the writing.

content.text: The plat or map: When a description calls for a plat and the parties acted with reference to it, the plat becomes part of the description as if recited in the deed, and its monuments have equal dignity with those in the writing. But if there is a clear error in the call for the map, or the parties did not actually act with reference to it, the map is treated as surplusage (unnecessary matter).

content.html:
Locating errors

If a description contains an error and the error can be isolated, the error is placed where it occurs — not smeared across the whole description. Before giving a distance or bearing control, the surveyor must investigate and eliminate the possibility of a mistake; the surveyor has an obligation to examine all reasonable avenues to rectify a discrepancy. When two factors conflict and nothing indicates which is correct, the first stated is preferred; when a number appears as both figures and words, the words control unless the contrary is proved.

content.text: Basis of bearings: Unless stated otherwise, every bearing in a present-day metes-and-bounds description is presumed to refer to the same basis at the same point, and each line's bearing is constant throughout its length. If one original line can be identified and was described by a measured magnetic bearing, the difference between the original record bearing and the present measured bearing is the correction to apply to the other record bearings of the same deed.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Construe the whole instrument to give effect to every term; particular descriptions control general ones; the earliest of equal rights is preferred.
'To' is a word of exclusion — the call after 'to' controls; a called monument controls, and its bearing/distance become controlling only if the monument is lost.
Parol evidence may explain an ambiguous PoB, show where a line was run, or show fraud — but may not add, subtract, or vary deed terms.
A plat the parties acted upon becomes part of the description; an erroneous or unrelied-upon plat is surplusage.
An isolable error is placed where it occurs; words control over conflicting figures; the first-stated factor is preferred when nothing else decides.

flashcards.definition: Oral evidence may not add to, subtract from, or vary the written terms of a deed; it may only locate an ambiguous PoB, show where a line was run/marked, or show fraud.
In a deed call, 'to' is a word of exclusion: the call preceding 'to' is informative and the call after 'to' is controlling.
Unnecessary matter in a description, such as a called plat the parties did not actually rely on or that contains a clear error; it is disregarded.
A maxim of construction: a specific (e.g., metes-and-bounds) expression qualifies and controls a general or vague one when they conflict.
When an error in a description can be isolated, it is placed where it occurs rather than distributed across the whole description.

If an original line is identified, the difference between its original record (magnetic) bearing and the present measured bearing is the correction applied to the other record bearings of the same deed.

images_needed.caption: A deed traverse where one identifiable line's record bearing differs from its measured bearing by a fixed rotation; the same rotation applied to all other courses.

lessons.title: Possession as Evidence and the Surveyor's Responsibility
learning_objectives: Explain how lines of possession relate to record boundaries.
State the limits of a surveyor's authority regarding unwritten rights.
Describe the surveyor's disclosure duty when occupation and record differ.

content.html:
When occupation differs from the record

Reality often clouds retracement: fences, walls, and cultivation lines (lines of occupation) frequently do not coincide with the record line built from the operative descriptions. Doctrines such as adverse possession, estoppel, practical location, acquiescence, and unwritten agreement can cause ownership to follow the occupation line rather than the record line. Lines of occupation are often held by courts to be the boundary where circumstances dictate.

Fact versus law — the surveyor's limit

Courts say: "where the boundaries are is a question of fact; what the boundaries are is a question of law." A surveyor is a fact finder. The surveyor may sift the facts and opine that the record boundary is in one place and the occupation line in another — but the surveyor cannot decide that the occupation line has become the ownership boundary by an equitable doctrine. That is a legal conclusion only a court can make. Even an attorney cannot, on their own motion, declare the occupation line the boundary; the procedure of litigation must be followed.

content.text: Liability: A surveyor who quietly monuments the occupation line as 'the boundary' may be liable for failing to disclose the conflict. Where occupation differs from record, the marketability of title is in doubt. The surveyor's duty is to DISCLOSE the difference — show both the record line and the line of occupation on the plat — and advise the client to seek legal advice.

content.html:
The reasonableness standard

The significance of a difference between fence and record depends on scale and context. A fence one foot off the record line around a 500-acre farm probably does not make title unmarketable; the same one-foot difference around a quarter-acre residential lot likely does. The surveyor weighs the extent, nature, and history of the occupation — but reports it as evidence, leaving the ownership determination to the court.

Unwritten agreement

An unwritten boundary agreement requires that (1) the adjoiners did not know where the true boundary was, (2) they agreed on a location and monumented it, and (3) they intended the marked line to be the true line. A surveyor has no authority to reform a deed without adjoiner approval; only a court can. Where the surveyor finds a provable unwritten agreement, the surveyor may only call attention to it, advise legal counsel, or attempt to have the adjoiners sign a written agreement — which converts the occupation line into a record line.

content.text: Texas connection: Texas applies the strict, statutory adverse-possession tracks (Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code Ch. 16) and the 'visible, open, exclusive, and unequivocal' possession standard (Madison v. Gordon). Acquiescence/agreed-boundary doctrines are applied narrowly, so the surveyor's measured record location is typically the starting point, with possession disclosed as evidence.

content.title: Key Takeaways
items: Lines of occupation can ripen into the ownership boundary through adverse possession, acquiescence, estoppel, or agreement.
Where the boundary is = question of fact (surveyor); what the boundary is = question of law (court).
A surveyor must disclose record-vs-occupation conflicts, not silently adopt the occupation line; otherwise liability and unmarketable title result.
Significance of a fence/record difference is judged by a reasonableness standard tied to scale and context.
A surveyor cannot reform a deed; a written boundary agreement among adjoiners turns occupation lines into record lines.

flashcards.definition: The physical line of use (fence, wall, cultivation edge); it may or may not coincide with the record boundary and can, through unwritten doctrines, become the ownership line.
'Where the boundaries are' is a question of fact for the surveyor; 'what the boundaries are' is a question of law for the court.
Title reasonably free from doubt and from claims that could form the basis of litigation; a fence/record conflict can render title unmarketable depending on scale.
A boundary fixed when adjoiners, uncertain of the true line, agree on and monument a location intending it as the true line; a surveyor can disclose it but not reform the deed.

images_needed.caption: A residential lot plat showing the deed/record line and an offset fence (line of occupation), both drawn, illustrating the surveyor's duty to disclose the gap rather than pick one.

quiz_questions.question_text: A surveyor retracing a sequential conveyance finds the original iron pin called for in the deed, but it sits 2.3 ft from the position computed by an exact modern coordinate solution. Which position controls the corner?
options: The found original iron pin, because the footsteps of the original surveyor control
The modern computed coordinate, because it is more accurate
The midpoint between the two positions
Whichever position gives the senior parcel its full record area
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The found original iron pin, because the footsteps of the original surveyor control
quiz_questions.explanation: Under the footsteps doctrine (Stafford v. King), an identifiable original monument controls over later, more precise measurements. Coordinates and modern computations are subordinate to the elements they were computed from. Splitting the difference and chasing record area both ignore the controlling found monument.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the three elements courts use to resolve deed conflicts may a surveyor NOT apply on the surveyor's own authority?
options: Unwritten/possession rights that have ripened into legal title
Senior rights
Written intentions of the parties
The dignity of calls
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Unwritten/possession rights that have ripened into legal title
quiz_questions.explanation: Validity of occupancy or property (unwritten) rights is a question of law for a court. A surveyor may apply senior rights and written intent (and the dignity of calls, which expresses intent), but may only disclose possible unwritten rights, not adjudicate them.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: The footsteps of the original surveyor, where they can be identified on the ground, override inconsistent field-note calls in the same description.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.explanation: True. Stafford v. King and Texas retracement doctrine hold that the original surveyor's actual lines and corners, where ascertainable, control even over inconsistent field-note calls. The dignity hierarchy is invoked only when the footsteps cannot be retraced.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario is the paradigm SIMULTANEOUS conveyance?
options: Lots created at one instant by a recorded subdivision plat
A landowner deeds off the west 5 acres in 2008, then the east 5 acres in 2015
A Republic-era headright patent carved from the public domain
An owner sells a metes-and-bounds tract, reserving the remainder
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Lots created at one instant by a recorded subdivision plat
quiz_questions.explanation: Subdivision lots are created simultaneously and have equal dignity (no seniority); excess/deficiency is apportioned. The other options describe sequential carve-outs created over time, which are governed by senior/junior rights.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a sequential conveyance, a measured shortage is found between two controlling monuments. How is the deficiency allocated between the senior and junior parcels?
options: The junior parcel absorbs the entire deficiency after the senior is made whole
The deficiency is prorated between senior and junior by record length
The senior parcel absorbs the deficiency because it is closer to the monument
The deficiency is split equally
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The junior parcel absorbs the entire deficiency after the senior is made whole
quiz_questions.explanation: In sequential conveyances senior rights govern: the senior takes its full description and the junior takes only the remainder, absorbing the whole excess or deficiency. Proration is reserved for simultaneous (equal-dignity) conveyances.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two adjoining sequential deeds, when plotted, leave a 4-ft-wide strip of ground described by neither deed. Who owns that gap?
options: The original grantor/seller, which may be the State
The senior parcel
The junior parcel
It is split between the two adjoining owners
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The original grantor/seller, which may be the State
quiz_questions.explanation: A gap (hiatus) is land that was never conveyed, so it remains with the original grantor; where the grantor was the sovereign, it may belong to the State. Overlaps (not gaps) go to the senior parcel.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: Because subdivision lots are 'simultaneous,' a surveyor can ignore the possibility of any senior right when retracing them.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.explanation: False. An original tract that was senior to others may later be subdivided, and that senior right survives. A title search back to the original formation of the conveyance is necessary to confirm no hidden senior right exists.

quiz_questions.question_text: Ambiguities in a patent issued by the State are resolved in favor of whom, and why?
options: The State, because a patent is a quitclaim-type deed giving no warranty
The patentee/grantee, because ambiguities are construed against the grantor
Whichever party recorded first
The adjoining landowner with the senior call
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The State, because a patent is a quitclaim-type deed giving no warranty
quiz_questions.explanation: State patents are treated as quitclaim deeds with no warranty, so ambiguities are resolved in favor of the State. This is the opposite of the ordinary private-deed rule, where ambiguities are construed against the grantor and for the grantee.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deed call to 'the line of the adjoining Smith tract' is generally treated as senior. When is that NOT a reliable indicator of seniority?
options: When the adjoining tract is itself junior, which only a title search to the original conveyance reveals
Never — an adjoiner call is always senior
Only when the adjoiner call also gives a bearing and distance
Only in simultaneous conveyances
quiz_questions.correct_answer: When the adjoining tract is itself junior, which only a title search to the original conveyance reveals
quiz_questions.explanation: An abutter call is normally senior but is not always a correct criterion: the adjoiner may itself be junior. A title search back to the original formation of the conveyance is required to determine seniority reliably.

quiz_questions.question_text: Rank these conflicting calls from HIGHEST to LOWEST dignity: distance, natural monument, area, artificial monument.
options: Natural monument > artificial monument > distance > area
Natural monument > distance > artificial monument > area
Artificial monument > natural monument > area > distance
Area > distance > artificial monument > natural monument
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Natural monument > artificial monument > distance > area
quiz_questions.explanation: The dignity order is natural monuments > artificial monuments > (adjoiner) > course > distance > area/quantity > coordinates. Natural objects are most reliable; area/quantity is among the weakest ('of no description' by itself).

quiz_questions.question_text: A deed call reads 'North 200.00 feet to a 2-inch iron pipe.' The pipe is found, called for, and undisturbed at 203.4 feet. Where is the corner?
options: At the found iron pipe (203.4 ft), because a called, identifiable, undisturbed monument controls over distance
At 200.00 ft, because the recorded distance is exact
At the average, 201.7 ft
Wherever the area closes correctly
quiz_questions.correct_answer: At the found iron pipe (203.4 ft), because a called, identifiable, undisturbed monument controls over distance
quiz_questions.explanation: An artificial monument that is called for, identifiable, and undisturbed controls over a conflicting distance. The bearing and distance are informative — they tell you where to look for the pipe. They would become controlling only if the pipe were lost.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: A call to a monument, although normally controlling, may be rejected when all the other terms of the deed show the monument call was inserted in error.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.explanation: True. The dignity hierarchy is a set of rebuttable presumptions subordinate to intent. A call that would defeat the parties' intentions is rejected regardless of its comparative dignity, including a monument call shown by the rest of the deed to be erroneous.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do coordinates rank at the very bottom of the dignity of calls?
options: Coordinates are computed from measurements of distance and angle and are therefore inferior to the elements they were computed from
Coordinates are always wrong
Coordinates are never recorded in deeds
Coordinates are senior to monuments
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Coordinates are computed from measurements of distance and angle and are therefore inferior to the elements they were computed from
quiz_questions.explanation: Coordinates are traditionally informational and are derived from underlying measurements, so they cannot be more reliable than those measurements. A precisely computed monument may be replaced by its coordinates, but the coordinate does not outrank a found monument.

quiz_questions.question_text: Under the parol evidence rule as applied to boundaries, parol evidence may be used to:
options: Establish the location of an ambiguous Point of Beginning, show where a line was run, or show fraud
Add a new course to the deed that the parties forgot
Increase the acreage to match the grantor's intent
Change a recorded bearing the surveyor believes is wrong
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Establish the location of an ambiguous Point of Beginning, show where a line was run, or show fraud
quiz_questions.explanation: Parol evidence may explain an ambiguity — locate an ambiguous PoB, show where the line was actually run and marked, or show fraud. It may NOT add, subtract, or vary the written terms of the deed.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the call '...thence to the centerline of Maple Street,' how does the word 'to' affect the calls?
options: 'To' is a word of exclusion: the call after 'to' (the centerline) controls and the call before it informs
'To' is a word of inclusion that adds both calls together
'To' means the distance controls over the monument
'To' has no legal effect
quiz_questions.correct_answer: 'To' is a word of exclusion: the call after 'to' (the centerline) controls and the call before it informs
quiz_questions.explanation: 'To' is a word of exclusion. The call preceding 'to' is informative; the call following 'to' (here, the road centerline, an artificial monument) is controlling.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deed contains both '12 acres' in figures and 'twelve and one-half acres' in words, and the conflict cannot otherwise be resolved. Which controls?
options: The words ('twelve and one-half acres'), unless the contrary is proved
The figures ('12 acres') always
Neither — area never controls anything
The smaller of the two values
quiz_questions.correct_answer: The words ('twelve and one-half acres'), unless the contrary is proved
quiz_questions.explanation: When a number appears as both figures and spelled-out words, the words control unless the contrary can be proved. (Note this is a tie-breaker for the quantity term itself; area/quantity remains a low-dignity element overall.)

quiz_questions.question_text: A surveyor is convinced a long-standing fence has become the true boundary by adverse possession. What is the proper action?
options: Disclose both the record line and the fence on the plat and advise the client to seek legal advice
Monument the fence as the boundary and omit the record line
Reform the deed to match the fence
Ignore the fence entirely
quiz_questions.correct_answer: Disclose both the record line and the fence on the plat and advise the client to seek legal advice
quiz_questions.explanation: Whether occupation has become ownership is a question of law for a court. The surveyor's duty is to disclose the conflict (show both lines) and advise legal counsel — not to adjudicate the doctrine or silently adopt the fence, which risks liability and clouds marketability.

quiz_questions.question_text: Complete the maxim courts use to define the surveyor's role: 'Where the boundaries ___ is a question of fact; what the boundaries ___ is a question of law.'
options: are; are
were; will be
start; end
close; open
quiz_questions.explanation: 'Where the boundaries are is a question of fact; what the boundaries are is a question of law.' The surveyor (fact finder) locates the boundary; the court decides the legal effect of unwritten doctrines.

quiz_questions.question_text: True or False: A one-foot difference between a fence and the record line has the same effect on marketability of title regardless of the size of the parcel.
options: True
False
quiz_questions.explanation: False. A reasonableness standard applies. A one-foot fence offset around a 500-acre farm likely does not make title unmarketable, while the same offset around a quarter-acre residential lot likely does.

quiz_questions.question_text: Grantor O owns a tract with 400 ft of record frontage. O deeds the west 240 ft to A (2009), then deeds 'the remainder' to B (2014). A resurvey between the controlling end monuments finds only 392 ft of frontage. How much frontage does B (junior) receive?
quiz_questions.explanation: Senior rights: A keeps the full 240 ft. B takes the remainder = 392 - 240 = 152 ft. The 8-ft deficiency falls entirely on the junior, B; it is not prorated.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deed call reads 'N 00° 00' E, 150.00 ft to a found stone monument.' The monument is found, called for, and undisturbed at a measured distance of 154.20 ft along that bearing. Treating the monument as controlling, what corrected distance (ft) should be used to reach the corner?
quiz_questions.explanation: The called, identifiable, undisturbed monument controls over the record distance, so the corrected distance is the measured distance to the monument, 154.20 ft. The record 150.00 ft is merely informative.

problem_templates.name: Junior remainder after senior call is satisfied
problem_templates.question_template: Grantor O owns a tract with {{rec:f2}} ft of record frontage along a road. O first deeds the senior parcel, a width of {{senior:f2}} ft, to A. O then deeds 'the remainder' to junior grantee B. A resurvey between the two controlling end monuments finds the true frontage is {{meas:f2}} ft. Applying senior rights (no proration), what frontage (ft) does junior parcel B receive?
problem_templates.explanation_template: Senior rights govern a sequential conveyance: A keeps its full {{senior:f2}} ft, and the junior B absorbs the entire excess/deficiency, taking the remainder = {{meas:f2}} − {{senior:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

images_needed.caption: Frontage strip with senior width fixed and junior remainder spanning to the measured end monument

problem_templates.name: Corrected distance when a monument controls over record distance
problem_templates.question_template: A line is described as bearing {{brg:dms}} for a record distance of {{rec:f2}} ft 'to a found {{mon}} monument.' The monument is called for, identifiable, and undisturbed; it is found along the same bearing at a measured horizontal distance of {{meas:f2}} ft. Treating the monument as the controlling call, what corrected distance (ft) reaches the corner, and by how much does it exceed the record? Report the corrected distance.

solution_steps_template.title: Adopt the measured distance to the monument
solution_steps_template.result_template: Corrected distance = {{meas:f2}} ft (record {{rec:f2}} ft is informative)

problem_templates.explanation_template: An artificial monument that is called for, identifiable, and undisturbed controls over a conflicting distance. The corrected distance is the measured distance to the monument, {{meas:f2}} ft; the record {{rec:f2}} ft becomes controlling only if the monument is lost.

images_needed.caption: A bearing line where the found monument sits beyond the record distance; corner held at the monument

problem_templates.name: Gap or overlap width between two adjoining deeds
problem_templates.question_template: A senior deed describes a parcel whose east line is at station {{e1:f2}} ft east of a common origin. The adjoining junior deed describes its west line at station {{w2:f2}} ft east of the same origin. Compute the width (ft) of the gap or overlap between the two deed lines (report a positive number for the magnitude).

solution_steps_template.title: Locate the two deed lines
solution_steps_template.calculation_template: Senior east line at {{e1:f2}} ft; junior west line at {{w2:f2}} ft

solution_steps_template.title: Width magnitude
solution_steps_template.result_template: |{{d:f2}}| = {{_answer}} ft (positive station diff = gap; negative = overlap)

problem_templates.explanation_template: If the junior west line lies east of the senior east line, the strip between them is a gap (belongs to the seller/State); if it lies west, the deeds overlap (the overlap belongs to the senior). The width is the absolute difference of the two stations = {{_answer}} ft.

images_needed.caption: Two adjoining deed lines on a common baseline with the gap (or overlap) width dimensioned between them

problem_templates.name: Bearing-distance to a corrected corner position (departure and latitude)
problem_templates.question_template: From a found controlling corner at coordinates (N {{n0:f2}}, E {{e0:f2}}), a deed line runs {{brg:dms}} (a bearing of {{az:f2}} degrees from north, in the NE quadrant) for the corrected distance of {{dist:f2}} ft to the next corner. Compute the EASTING (E) of the next corner.
problem_templates.explanation_template: For a NE-quadrant bearing, the departure (change in easting) is dist × sin(bearing angle) = {{dist:f2}} × sin({{az:f2}}°) = {{dep:f2}} ft. Adding to the starting easting {{e0:f2}} gives E = {{_answer}} ft. (The northing would use dist × cos of the same angle.)

images_needed.caption: A single course from a found corner with the bearing angle, departure, and latitude triangle drawn to the next corner

homework.title: Homework 1 — Sequential Conveyance Retracement and Conflict Resolution
homework.prompt:
**Scenario.** In 1962 landowner O owned a single tract fronting 600.00 ft (record) along the north line of County Road 7, the tract running 300.00 ft deep. O made the following conveyances in order:

- **1962 — Parcel A (senior):** the west 200.00 ft of frontage, full depth, 'thence N 00° 00' E 300.00 ft to a 1-inch iron pipe set this survey.'
- **1968 — Parcel B:** the next 200.00 ft of frontage east of A, full depth.
- **1975 — Parcel C (junior):** 'the remainder of the O tract lying east of Parcel B,' full depth.

**Field data (your resurvey).** Between the two original controlling road-corner monuments (found, undisturbed), the measured frontage is **592.40 ft**. The 1-inch iron pipe called for at the NW interior corner is found, identifiable, and undisturbed, but it lies **N 00° 00' E 304.10 ft** from the road corner (record was 300.00 ft).

**Tasks.**
1. Identify whether these are sequential or simultaneous conveyances and state which rule (senior rights vs. apportionment) governs the frontage discrepancy.
2. Compute the frontage each of A, B, and C receives. Show that the deficiency falls on the junior parcel only.
3. For Parcel A's west line, state whether the corner is held at the found iron pipe (304.10 ft) or at the record 300.00 ft, and justify your answer using the dignity of calls and the footsteps doctrine.
4. Suppose a later survey reveals the 1975 deed to C and a 1977 deed to a neighbor both describe a 3.0-ft-wide strip on C's east side (an overlap). State who owns the overlap and the principle that decides it.
5. Cite *Stafford v. King* for one proposition you relied on, and note one Texas-specific consideration (GLO patent seniority or TBPELS retracement duty).

images_needed.caption: Plan of the original O tract subdivided into Parcels A, B, C along County Road 7, showing the two found road-corner monuments, the measured 592.40 ft frontage, and the found iron pipe on A's west line.

homework.title: Homework 2 — Dignity of Calls and the Surveyor's Disclosure Duty (written + applied)
homework.prompt:
**Part A — Dignity analysis.** For each call conflict below, state which element controls and why, and identify which call is merely informative:

1. 'N 45° 00' E 100.00 ft to the centerline of Mill Creek' — the creek centerline is found 6 ft short of the platted distance.
2. 'thence along the east line of the Jones Survey' vs. a recited bearing of 'S 02° W' that diverges 1° from the Jones line.
3. 'containing 5.00 acres, more or less' vs. monumented corners that compute to 4.82 acres.
4. A coordinate listed for a corner vs. a found, called-for concrete monument 0.4 ft away.

**Part B — Parol evidence.** A deed's Point of Beginning is described only as 'the old oak at the corner of the Smith place,' and the oak is gone. List what parol evidence the surveyor MAY use to re-establish the PoB and what parol evidence may NOT be used.

**Part C — Possession/responsibility memo.** You retrace a quarter-acre residential lot and find a board fence that encroaches 1.6 ft onto the record line for its full length; the fence has stood for 22 years. Write a short (150–250 word) professional memo to your client explaining: (i) why you will not simply monument the fence as the boundary, (ii) the fact-vs-law distinction, (iii) the marketability concern, and (iv) your recommended next step. Reference the reasonableness standard and the surveyor's disclosure duty.

images_needed.caption: Residential lot with the deed/record line and a parallel fence offset 1.6 ft inside it, dimensioned, to support the disclosure memo.
